PODCAST GLOSSARY
If you’re going to become an expert, you’ll need to understand the lingo.
It is recommended that you read and know these terms as you’ll hear them often…
COMMON TERMS
Referring to the general background noise in the room you’re recording in. Also
sometimes referred to as the room tone.
AMBIENT SOUND
A short piece of audio, usually used to split up certain parts of the show. Can be an
intro, an outro, or a promo in the middle. Sometimes referred to as a "stinger".
BUMPER
Refers to one individual show on a podcast.
EPISODE
A website/service you'll sign up and create an account with when you launch your
show.
HOSTING
A short piece of musical audio usually used to introduce your show or to introduce
different segments of your show.
JINGLE
While creating a podcast is a lot of work, it is worth it.
Unlike other social media content which is only valuable
for a few days, a podcast can be downloaded for years.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2022 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
5
Information attached to your mp3 file, such as the podcast title, episode title, your
name as the producer, cover art etc.
METADATA (ID3 TAGS)
An advertisement which appears in the middle of media content.
MID-ROLL AD
A common term for editing your podcast together.
MIXING
A type of audio file, and the type to use to distribute your podcast episodes. MP3s are
smaller audio files.
MP3 FILE
Displays hundreds of podcasts to potential listeners so that people can find and listen
to your show.
PODCAST DIRECTORIES
A network of podcasts connected by a similar theme, genre, partnership, or ownership.
PODCAST NETWORK
Refers to all editing and activity that happens after the original audio recording.
POST-PRODUCTION
An advertisement that appears at the end of a piece of media content.
POST-ROLL AD
An advertisement that appears at the beginning of a piece of media content.
PRE-ROLL AD
While we’ll teach you how to monetize your podcast,
don’t think of it as a sales channel. Podcasts are the
greatest form of free marketing and traffic ever created.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

6
An individual link you’ll get from your media host when you sign up with them.
RSS FEED
This is essentially the same as a season of a television programme. It's a way of
separating your content into smaller self-contained blocks.
SEASON
A single stream of recorded sound with no location in a sound field.
TRACK
Refer to short previews of a piece of content, which demonstrates scenes and the
general plot and mood of a piece of media, in order to garner interest.
TRAILER
Refers to the number of users who answer an advertisement's call to action like request
to like or subscribe, to visit a webpage, or check out the content provider on other
services.
USER ENGAGEMENT
Refers to the ability of a product or service to retain its audience over a given period
of time.
USER RETENTION
A type of audio file format that is only playable on Apple devices and software.
AAC
Usually a piece of music that you run underneath someone talking as background
material.
BED
While you may come across these, you can primarily use this list as a reference…
TECHNICAL TERMS
Smart people with money are way more likely to listen
to podcasts than to use social platform like TikTok or
Instagram. 1 listener can be worth 10,000 social “likes”.

7
Measured in Kilobits per second (or Kbps). This is how many bits of data are in a single
second of audio.
BIT RATE
When the audio is so loud that this waveform spikes so that it touches the edge of the
recording window.
CLIPPING
The process of getting the volume be more balanced throughout your recording.
COMPRESSION
Microphones that uses a phantom power source or battery to work. They are more
sensitive and, as a result, pick up way more of the audio in an environment.
CONDENSER MICROPHONE
The number of bits of information in each sample, and it directly corresponds to the
resolution of each sample. The bit depth will often be in 16, 24 and 32.
BIT DEPTH
Short for equaliser or equalisation. Here you can adjust certain elements of different
frequencies of your audio.
EQ
A filter that reduces or eliminates any low frequencies from your audio.
HIGH-PASS FILTER
An audio interface is similar to a mixer, that acts as a bridge between your microphone
and your recording platform.
INTERFACE
A setting where you adjust the sensitivity of your microphone.
GAIN
Don’t be scared by the technical aspects of a podcast.
We’ll walk you through it step-by-step, and once you’re
up and running, all the technical stuff is super simple.

8
Refers to the delay of the audio signal in going in and out of a system.
LATENCY
This is the result when pieces of frequencies at the high and low end are lost during
compression.
LOSSY AUDIO
A microphone that is commonly known as a lapel mic.
LAVALIERE
This filter reduces or eliminates any high frequencies from your audio.
LOW-PASS FILTER
An audio interface that allows you to mix together other elements into your show as
you record, rather than editing them in the post-production phase. This is more
advanced and usually requires a professional.
MIXER
The audio file after you have mixed all your audio together and you’re exporting it out.
MIX DOWN
A single stream of recorded sound with no location in a sound field.
MONO
Or multitracking; when you work with multiple audio track during the editing process.
MULTITRACK
An option you will find commonly in your DAW that alters the size of your waveform,
meaning it will adjust the levels of the audio based on the original waveforms peaks
and troughs.
NORMALISATION
While I recommend recording your podcast as video, the
most important aspect is the audio. You’ll want to get a
good mic and record somewhere that doesn’t have an echo.

9
When your peaks go a little too high, generally because of a louder noise, like an
exciting comment or a cough.
PEAKING
Additional features you can install on your DAW or website to give you extra control and
customisation options.
PLUG-INS
Patterns which change how the microphone picks up audio. Every microphone has one
or multiple polar patterns.
POLAR PATTERNS
Measures how many data points of a sound wave in comparison to the frame rate of
the piece. Sample rates are measured in ‘hertz' or ‘Hz', which means cycles per second.
SAMPLE RATE
The audio is split across the left and right channel.
STEREO AUDIO
This is the high-quality recording of your audio that sounds exactly as it was recorded.
WAV FILE
A type of electrical connector primarily used in professional audio/visual and stage
lighting equipment. XLR stands for "External Line Return".
XLR
The act of recording and storing data to listen, watch or read later on.
TIMESHIFTING
One of the things you need to realize about podcasts is how
fun they can be. Getting started is the hardest part, but
once you get going, it is all about sharing your passion.

10
INTRODUCTION TO PODCASTS
If video killed the radio star, then podcasts definitely brought it back!
The popularity and growth of podcasts is astounding. 57% of the online population
now listens to podcasts regularly. What’s even better is the fact that those listeners
have money. The average income of podcast listeners is higher than on any other
medium, and their level of engagement is unparalleled. 65% of listeners report they
are likely to purchase a product they heard on a podcast.
Podcasters love podcasts because it’s a way for them to develop deep meaningful
relationships with their audience, unlike other social media platforms. There are
many reasons to get into podcasting: receiving exposure, gaining credibility,
generating leads or exploring passion. Podcasts can be highly profitable both as a
guest and as a host.
This manual will teach you how to maximize your results from both. By the time you
complete this manual and the supporting materials, you’ll be able to reach more
people in less time than you ever could before.
WHY PODCASTING

avid listener. Download a variety of podcasts so you can

11
Remember that people listen to podcasts on the go. They are
in their car, working out or mowing the lawn. Provide several
easy to remember “call-to-actions” throughout your content.
While podcasts can take many forms, you can think of them as a modern day talk radio
show that can go anywhere. Podcasts can be streamed online, downloaded to your
phone and often viewed video content. While a podcast can be recorded as either video
or audio, the audio portion is what ultimately becomes the podcast file.
Most people browse and listen to podcasts through an app on their smartphone.
iPhone users usually use the “Podcasts” app or iTunes. Android users will often use
Google Play Music or the Google Podcasts app. There are also third party applications
that you can use to listen to podcasts. Spotify is the most popular, followed by Stitcher.
However you listen to your podcasts, you can browse through hundreds of titles and
categories. Everything from news, comedy, business, true crime, sports, culture etc.
Once you find one you like, you can subscribe and get notified every time a new
episode becomes available. You can also have the episode automatically downloaded
on your phone so you can listen to it on demand wherever you are.

PREPARING YOUR PODCAST
PRELIMINARY RESEARCH
Now that you get the basics of podcasting, it will help to start exploring what is out
there. Your goal here is really three fold…
The last part is important, as you’ll likely get your best ideas from podcasts that are
completely unrelated to you. Branch out and explore for fresh ideas.
When doing competitive research, simply pick a few of the networks such as Apple,
Spotify and Stitcher. You’ll notice that users can find shows with both a search bar as
well as a category-based structure. Use both options because it will help you
understand the user experience.
Understand what you’re competitors are doing
Get ideas from a wide variety of industries
Decide how you can make your podcast unique

define you. You will create your own unique style. You’ve got
to be different or you’ll never stand out from the crowd.

Don’t be nervous about getting bad reviews. If you don’t
have haters, you haven’t made it big. One of my secrets to
success is embracing my critics and letting them fuel me.
Ultimately, you’ll want to make sure that your podcast is placed in a category where
your ideal audience will find you. While this is usually obvious, sometimes you will
have a few options. In that case, take into consideration the level of competition
and how your show could potentially differ from theirs. When researching
podcasts, here are some key factors to pay attention to:
You will get a lot of value by reading the comments on your competitors’ podcasts.
A lot of the time, you will be able to get a good feel of what people like and don’t like
about the episodes. Depending on the content, sometimes the goal is to get opposite
reactions to what they are discussing. In that situation, bad comments aren’t
necessarily a bad thing.
Once you’ve browsed the podcasts universe, it’s time to start downloading. Make sure
to listen to a variety of shows, both in your niche and outside. Make it a habit to listen
to podcasts whenever you’re driving, doing dishes or any other free time.
Podcast Title - Does it include a keyword? Create curiosity?
Show Description - Is it persuasive? What is unique?
Cover Artwork - Does it grab attention? Feel professional?

Listener Comments - Are they getting people talking?

DEFINING YOUR NICHE
NICHES AND NAMING
One of the biggest things that people are uncertain about is how narrowly to define
their niche. While there is no perfect equation, it is good to take into consideration
the potential factors involved.
The exception to the rule is if you already have a following. Grant likes to go for larger
audiences and broad topics, but his listeners are extremely devoted. How niche you
go will also depend on your overall goal. Let’s look at a real world example:
The audience for apartment investing is much smaller than that of general real estate
investing. If your goal is to create a network of high end investors with deep pockets
to buy your deals, then it could pay to get really specific.
BROAD NICHE 	NARROW NICHE
Less Devoted
Harder to Target
Larger Audience
More Devoted
Easier to Target
Smaller Audience
Broad Niche Example: real estate investing
Narrow Niche Example: multi-family apartment investing
PRO TIP: If you want to gauge the level of interest for a topic, do a Google
Trends search. If you can find something that is hot or currently doesn’t have
a good podcast, that might be your niche!
If you haven’t defined your niche yet, it helps to know what people are looking for.
Search term: Grant Cardone

18
NAMING YOUR PODCAST
Regardless of what the research says, remember that your
audience are real people. Each person tuning in is an
individual, so create for your listener, not for a search engine.
This short-term show got instant results, partly because the name was so searchable
Once you’ve committed to creating a podcast in a particular niche, you’ll need to
give it a name. Ideally, the name should be something catchy or memorable.
For example, the podcast name “The Cardone Zone” is great because it sticks in
your head.
That said, assuming you don’t have the reputation that Grant has, you should try to
be descriptive with your title. Make sure people will know what your show is about.
Also, if you can include your most ideal keyword phrase, it will help people find your
podcast when searching for the topic.
Realistically, it is tough to achieve this all in a title of less than 36 characters (the max
that Apple shows in their directory). To help with the many podcasts will also have a
tagline, or a secondary title. Adding a tagline such as, “Money. Finance. Career.” for
example, gives a really clear picture of what the podcast is about.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

BRANDING AND DESIGN
In order to effectively brand your podcast, you need to know who you’re talking to. A
good place to start is to develop a listener avatar. By creating a very specific avatar,
that even has a fictitious name, you will gain a much better understanding of how
you should format, plan and market your podcast.

PODCAST ARTWORK
After completing the exercise and defining a character, you will have much more
clarity in every decision you make. You may find it valuable to create two or three
different avatars, but try not to create more than that.
The first thing that a potential listener will see is your show’s image, sometimes
referred to as the cover image.
Make sure that it is eye-catching and that any text is very easy to read, even when
the image is small. It can be helpful to include an image of the host’s face, especially
if they are smiling. Notice that on the images below without people they leave a lot
of negative (empty) space. This can sometimes draw as much attention as the space
that you use.

PODCAST DESCRIPTIONS
One of the most important factors when it comes to your podcast being
downloaded is the description and summary. Write about who your podcast is for
and why they should listen. They should get a clear picture after reading your
description of what they can expect from listening to one of your episodes.
Grant’s description on the Stitcher directory

23
EPISODE FORMAT
Remember to add descriptive keywords to your description so people searching for
those terms can more easily find them. It is important to note that you can always go
back and edit your description, so it doesn’t have to be perfect the first time.
Once you have a general plan and have identified your listener avatar, you can start
to lay out what your podcast episodes consist of. While you can format your podcast
in countless ways, it can help to think about your show in 3 different sections:
Note that this general format will change if you have sponsors. A common
structure when advertising is added is to include pre-roll and mid-roll clips of
30 to 60 second ads.
With each episode, you want to create excitement and provide a clear value
proposition. You want to tell the listener what they stand to gain by listening
to the episode. The excitement is generally enhanced by using music and a
standard show intro.
Here is a common formula for achieving this…
Intro	1
INTRO	1
Main Segment	2 	Outro	3
Most basic format for any type of podcast episode
Potential episode format if you sell paid advertising
Pre-Roll Ad	A 	Intro	1
Outro	3
Main Segment	2
Main Segment	2 	Mid-Roll Ad	B
Start with a short sound bite highlight from the show (creates intrigue)
Host provides quick episode summary (benefits, problems it solves, etc)
Play same music intro played on every episode (short and high energy)

-- 23 of 65 --

24
© 2022 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
Far and away the most important part of your podcast is
the intro. If you don’t grab people’s attention right away,
the rest is a waste of time. Be sure to come out swinging.
While you always want to have a plan, I definitely work best
when I keep it natural. Create a framework to give yourself
confidence, but don’t be afraid to mix it up in the moment.
Remember, it is all about the listener. State a key problem and tell them specifically
how the episode is going to help them solve that problem.
The main section of your podcast episode will vary greatly depending on the
style of show that you are producing.
Regardless of what type of podcast you do, you’ll want to create a plan to
create a standardized format. Listeners do like when you mix things up, but if
you don’t provide some level of consistency your show will seem disjointed
and unprofessional. People are creatures of habit. Consider adding consistent
questions, fun segments or a signature “catch phrase”.
The beautiful thing about podcasts is that they don’t need to fit into a
30 minute TV block. There are podcasts that can last 4 hours and there are
podcasts that last 10 minutes. Regardless, people do appreciate shows having
some level of consistency so they plan their lives around it.
At the end of the day, each episode should be as long as it needs to be. The
key is you don’t need to fill your episode with meaningless content to make it
last longer, nor do you need to cut valuable content to get it down to a certain
length. Just make it the perfect length to provide your intended value.
MAIN SEGMENT	2
Common Question: How long should my podcast be?

There are successful podcasts that release daily, weekly, monthly, bi-monthly,
weekdays… you name it. In addition to frequency, you need to choose a day and time
to consistently upload. For example, your listeners might get in the habit of listening
to it on the way to work Wednesday morning.
I am very big on giving people an easy web address to find
me and buy my stuff. We always have a specific call-to-action
and create custom links at grantcardone.com/whatever.
Often overlooked, the outro is a very important part of your podcast. If a
listener reaches the end, it means that they likely enjoyed your content. This
gives a great opportunity to give the user one or multiple call-to-actions.
These may include…
The outro can either be recorded as part of the original podcast broadcast, or
you can record it afterwards. If you add the outro in post production you will
be able to add background music and really get it right.
OUTRO	3
Subscribe to your show
Leave a review and rating
Visit website or sales funnels
Get special deal limited deal
Check you out on social media
Preview the next episode

What people don’t realize about me is how much content I’ve

EPISODE PLANNING
Another factor in deciding how often you should release new episodes is your
schedule. You might be really gung-ho at first, but soon realize how much work it is
and lose steam. Be sure you don’t over commit on frequency. This should be fun, and
if it becomes a grind, you’re at risk of throwing in the towel.
Once you’ve decided on a consistent schedule, you’ve got to put your plan into
action. It is essential with podcasting that you plan ahead and stay extremely
organized. You can do this by using tools like Google Spreadsheets and Google
Calendar. For example…
You’ll always want to have a few recorded episodes ready to release at any time. In
other words, don’t put yourself in a situation where you don’t have an episode in the
case that something comes up. There are many unforeseen circumstances and
guests do cancel.
Create a list of potential topics
Create a list of potential guests
Create a calendar for recording

CREATING YOUR PODCAST
PODCAST EQUIPMENT
One of the first decisions you’ll need to make is whether you want to record your
podcast as video or as audio.
When you record as video, you can easily separate the audio into a separate file. The
obvious advantage of video is that it allows you to post on YouTube and use clips of
the video for other purposes. The only potential downfall is that it requires a camera,
not only for you, but for your guests as well.
We will assume that you will record as video, but if you prefer to just do audio that is
fine as well. Let’s look at a few potential recording setup options…
If you’re just getting started, and you don’t have a recording studio, you’ll likely want
to keep things as simple as possible. The most important piece of equipment is
always your microphone. The good news is that if you have a reliable computer, you
can use a mic that plugs directly into a USB port.
BASIC SETUP
USB cable 	XLR cable

The major difference between the basic setup and this one is the type of mic used.
Professional mics use what is called an XLR cable instead of a USB. While this allows
for higher quality sound, it does require more equipment (as you can see below).
PROFESSIONAL SETUP
XLR mics require the use of “audio interface” equipment, while USB mics do not.
Regarding your camera, many modern laptops and desktops have quality webcams
built in. If you need to buy an external webcam, virtually any model will do.

29
Other Professional Equipment:
Audio Interface
This is the equipment that XLR mics plugs into. An audio
interface will allow you to adjust audio levels and may
include other features. You will want an audio interface
that can feed directly into your computer with a USB cable
(most do).
Podcast Recorder
An alternative option to an audio interface is a podcast
recorder. Unlike audio interfaces, a podcast recorder
does not require a computer. They can even run on
batteries, making them a great option for recording
episodes on the go.
Camera
If you record with a streaming service such as Zoom, it is
easiest to buy an HD webcam that plugs into your
computer. If you’re recording a high production show
that isn’t streamed, you can upgrade to any DSLR model
camera.
Headphones
If you are recording audio only, or if you are extremely
concerned with sound quality, you may consider wearing
headphones. They will allow you to be aware of audio levels
and any potential issues. The only downside is the look.
NOTE: If you have multiple hosts in the same location you will definitely want to use
either an audio interface or a podcast recorder.
While there is lots of fancy equipment you can buy, you
don’t need to. The content is what matters the most. As
long as you get good quality sound, you’ll be just fine.
© 2022 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 29 of 65 --

30
Pop Filter
These are simple filters placed in front of the microphone to
help avoid unwanted noise. They typically attach directly to
the mic. While often used by professionals, they aren’t
essential. Just be sure they don’t block your face for video.
Any of this equipment can be found with a quick Amazon search. For example, “audio
interface for podcasts”. Generally speaking, you get what you pay for. Most brands will
be adequate for a low production show, but try to avoid getting the cheapest model of
anything.
Again, if you use the basic setup discussed above, none of this will be needed.
RECORDING STUDIO SETUP
You can buy all the best equipment, but if your recording environment is not on
point, you’ll look totally unprofessional. There are 3 key elements for setting up a
proper studio…
Keep the background relatively simple, unless you are building a full studio like
Grant’s. You can purchase a professional backdrop or create your own custom scene.
Example home-style backgrounds of professional creators

Unless you own a well established, and well funded business,
resist the temptation to build out a fancy studio right away. If
people like your content, they won’t care too much.
Ideally, have some space between you and the background. Avoid having distracting
items in the scene such as light switches, reflective surfaces or complex patterns.
Plants and bookshelves are generally good.
While the background is important, even more important is the subject being filmed
(you!) Your goal is to avoid unwanted glare or shadows on your face. The diagram
below shows a basic setup, but ultimately you can just play with your configuration
until you get it right.
You will likely want to buy two small LED video lights with adjustable settings. The
LED count should be between 60 (desktop) to 168 (small room).

Sound quality is essential, and even a great mic can’t fix an echoey room. Sound
bounces off hard surfaces so anything soft makes a difference. Carpet and drapes
help, or you can purchase soundproofing foam to stick on walls. You could even build
a fort using blankets for a childhood flashback!

for your guests. Zoom has all the basic features needed including split

version). The free version doesn’t have cloud recording and is limited to 40
minute recording sessions.

recording of everything. There is nothing worse than

It is important that you make the recording process as easy as possible on your
guests. Send them clear instructions beforehand that includes the meeting link,
what streaming service you use and whether or not they will be on video.
Professional Option:
SquadCast is a web based subscription software that delivers super high
quality “lossless” audio. Like Zoom, it also allows you to record video, but
provides separate audio and video files by default. If you are hosting VIP
guests, you might consider using a premium service like this.
Audacity is the long-time industry standard for audio recording software,
likely because it is free. While it is not the most modern of programs,
recording is relatively simple and it also serves as an editing tool as well.

34
As cliche as it may sound, it all comes down to being yourself
and not worrying what people think. Nobody has any
expectations of you, so let loose and let the haters hate.
SUBSTANCE TIPS
VOCAL TIPS
The host can make or break a podcast. Like anything, it is a learned skill that will
come with practice. The good news is that there are successful hosts of all
personality types, so you have it in you.
People will listen to just about anything if they are engaged. Whatever you do, avoid
being monotone and dull. Nothing makes people check out faster. Here are 3 ways
to keep it interesting…
PERFORMANCE TIPS
Share Stories
There is nothing that captivates people like a good story. Always come in
with at least one good relevant story in mind. Using analogies can also be
powerful. Anything that puts concepts into context and evoke emotion.
Have an Outline
You never want to come into a podcast and wing it. You also don’t want to
be too rigid. Unless you’re reading a script, as you might for a fictional
podcast, you will want to give yourself some flexibility. Bullet points are
usually good.
Be the Guide
Your goal is to bring people in and take them on an adventure with you.
Give your audience a name to associate with (i.e. “10Xers”). Do whatever
you can to put yourself in the listeners shoes and don’t be afraid to be
vulnerable.
© 2022 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 34 of 65 --

35
Learn to feel when your show is getting a little boring and
needs a boost. Interject a “pattern interrupt” by stressing an
important point with volume to shake people awake.
Don’t be afraid to challenge a guest. As long as it comes
from a good place, most smart people prefer real questions
to the same boring stuff they get asked all the time.
INTERVIEWING TIPS
Non-Verbal Expression
Regardless of whether or not you are on video, your non-verbal
communication comes through. It is true that when you smile, people
can feel it. Keep your body active as you talk to keep the energy up.
Pitch and Tone Variety
Speaking is way more than the words you say. Take people on a ride
vocally. When you are sharing something happy, your pitch and tone
should go up. When sharing something upsetting, they should fall.
Cadence and Pauses
The speed of your words also expresses emotion. For example, speaking
quickly creates excitement. A dramatic pause can be very powerful. Also
learn to repeat key phrases to drive home a point.
Do Your Research
Watch their interviews, review their website and buy their book. Do
anything to discover interesting facts and stories to work into your
conversation. Putting in the effort goes a long way with your guests.
Ask Great Questions
The quality of your questioning will dictate the quality of the interview.
Get your guests to go deep. Rather than simply reading questions, create
a healthy banter. For example, “Tell us about the time you…”
© 2022 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 35 of 65 --

36
Support Your Guest
Remember that your guest has their own agenda. Show appreciation by
helping them in tastefully promoting their stuff. Let your guest and
audience know that you’ll talk about it at the end of the show.
EDITING STRATEGY
Once you are done recording your first podcast, it is now time to edit. The first thing
to decide is whether you want to do it yourself, or outsource the work.
You do have several options to handle the heavy lifting for you. You can outsource
the work to an individual on places like Fiverr or Upwork. Alternatively, there are also
established companies that focus solely on providing podcast services.
EDITING YOUR PODCAST
While you can save a few bucks doing it yourself, I will
always hire the experts. It not only saves time, you learn
a ton and it allows you to create friends in the industry.

editing, social sharing and show note creation. Unlike other

You can even hire them to help you launch your podcast.

Best Self-Editing Option:
Descript is a web-based tool that edits both video and
audio. It is unique in that it transcribes your audio or video
into a text file. Then you simply edit the text by deleting
words or sections, and it automatically edits your media file.
It can even removes any “um’s” or “ahh’s” for you, which is a
real time saver… 10xresource.com/resonate
It is important to note that even if you do outsource your
editing, you will still need to familiarize yourself with the
basics of audio or video files. It is always a good idea to use
a file sharing service like Google Drive or Dropbox to stay
organized.
Whether you use Descript, or a traditional downloadable software like Audacity, it’s
going to take a little practice. After a few episodes, it will come much easier.
When editing audio files, the order in which you work will save a tremendous
amount of time…
NOTE: In order to be able to properly edit audio quality, every speaker should be on

You will get 80% of your results from the 20% of your effort.

downloads for Grant’s shows. Spotify is the fastest growing and brings a world of
music lovers who might not otherwise find your podcast.

Grant always says that “if you don’t track it, it won’t grow.”
Your statistics will be provided by your hosting provider and are very easy to access and
understand. With podcasts, there is only one key metric to keep an eye on: downloads.
It is important to realize that the term “download” is used to describe anytime someone
listens to an episode, regardless if it is streamed or downloaded.
While we will teach you strategies to get more downloads, the key to sustained growth
is getting people to subscribe. When someone subscribes to your podcast, your new
episodes will automatically be added to their podcast player. The spikes you see above
are from subscribers on the day new episodes are released.
DOWNLOADS AND SUBSCRIBERS
PODCAST STATISTICS
While you should always focus on growing your numbers, try not
to lose track of the fact that your downloads are real people.
Don’t get deterred, because every listener counts.

While downloads are the most important stat to track internally, the number of ratings
and reviews you receive are your most important public stats. Apple is the only major

subscribe, share, and leave you a review if listening on Apple.

Both the quantity and quality of your reviews will help you get discovered.

Nobody knows the exact equation for getting featured, or if there even is an equation.
That said, the requirements to put yourself in contention are pretty simple…
Here are some tips to help you get it done…
Top New Shows
Apple features a wide variety of shows on their main page.
Get as many listeners as fast as you can
Get those listeners to download and subscribe
Get as many positive reviews as possible
Clearly map out your launch - Don’t rush to get your first episode live
until you’re ready to fully promote it. You’ll want to get your launch date
in the books and have every step laid out to ensure you hit it hard.
Go live with three episodes - Since it is essential to have as many
downloads as possible, posting multiple podcasts on day one can help.
Depending on how serious you are about the goal, and how much
content you have, you may consider starting with four or five.
Focus on only one channel - While you’ll want to have your podcast
everywhere, if you want to get featured, you may not want to focus on
other channels at first. For example, if you promote Spotify at the same
time as you promote Apple, you’ll reduce your total Apple downloads and
therefore your chances of being featured.

Set a target for how many downloads you want and then
build your subscribers to get there. Build a relationship with
your audience because if they vibe, they will subscribe.
While the majority of your marketing activity will happen after each episode, there are
some things you can prepare to promote your overall show.
Much like a movie trailer, people often like to get a sample of what to expect before
jumping into a podcast. Some directories such as Spotify allow you to add a trailer which
is featured above your episodes. Trailers are generally between thirty seconds and three
minutes in length.
PROMOTIONAL MATERIALS
PROMO PIECE #1 	Podcast Trailer
Really nail your first episode - You only get one chance at a first
impression. If people like the first episode, they will listen to more. Put
your best recorded content as episode #1.
Promote every way you can - You’ll really need to use every resource you
have available. If you have marketing lists such as email and text, be sure
to hit them. Get help from your friends and family. Don’t be afraid to ask
your podcast guests to help too.
Get creative with reviews - Since the engagement from listeners is
important, consider doing something to encourage reviews. For example,
if you have an email list, you might run a contest for the best review.
Invest in some paid traffic - If you’re serious about the goal, spend some
money wisely for a week or two. Definitely do Google Search ads for any
term that has your industry and the word podcast. Try Facebook ads as
well. The more targeted the better.

When creating a post for your episode, you can design the page however you like. We

POSTING ON YOUR WEBSITE

content management platform. You can either use the

Beyond your website, you will also want a landing page to send
listeners to. Make the page simple, with an incentive to opt in.
Also use a domain name that is easy to remember.
The term show notes refers to the information you provide on your website about an
episode. Show notes can have several benefits. In addition to helping visitors on your
site learn more about a particular episode, they also give your listeners a reason to
visit your website.
While there is no exact formula, your show notes may include…
CREATING SHOW NOTES
� Outline and/or highlights of the episode
� Notable quotes from what is said during the show
� Optionally include timestamps to key points
� Include short bios on both the host and guests
� Links to resources mentioned during the show
� Images of guest from the show recorded
� Transcriptions of the entire audio (rev.com)
� Promotion of your products or opt-in pages 	Add big download buttons!
While show notes are great content, don’t waste too much
energy on them. Save time by utilizing the notes you used for
recording your podcast or hire a service to do them for you.
Again, don’t forget that the most important things to include on your
website are an audio player and links to podcast directories. Your goal is
to get people to subscribe!

CONTENT REPURPOSING
There are a number of ways that you can utilize your podcast content. Here are some
tips on how to repurpose your footage into new formats, and where to share the
content…
Content Format: Full Length Video
Share On: YouTube, Instagram TV
Details: Use the entire edited show with custom edits
Advantage: People can follow and become real fans.
Content Format: Short 1-Minute Video
Share On: Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts, LinkedIn
Details: Vertical oriented video with 60-second highlight
Advantage: Reels and Shorts are highly discoverable.
Content Format: Multi-Part Video Stories
Share On: Instagram Stories, Facebook Stories
Details: Add multiple 15-second clips strung together
Advantage: Get lots of views and allow outbound links.
Content Format: Image with Guest Quote
Share On: Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn
Details: Combines good one-liner from show with image
Advantage: Simple and shows respect for your guest.

When you appear on someone else’s podcast, be sure to
confirm that you can also use the content for your own social
media. Most hosts are cool, but it is better to ask in advance.
Appearing on other people’s podcasts is not only free publicity, it targets the perfect
audience: podcast listeners! Here are some tips and strategies you can use to get on
other podcasts.
To find potential shows you can simply browse the podcast directories or do
some basic Google searches. You will obviously want to appear on podcasts
within your niche, but also think of parallel industries where you can reach a new
audience. Be sure to stay organized with the shows that could be a potential fit.
Keeping appearances in a simple spreadsheet is ideal.
APPEARING ON PODCASTS
STEP 1 	Find the Right Podcast to Pitch
Podchaser is the unofficial directory of everything podcasts. In addition to
searching shows, you can also see what guests have appeared on what
podcasts. Claim your Podchaser profile to keep your show details and
appearances up to date. Podchaser also offers several paid services
including data and review management. 10xresource.com/podchaser
Before reaching out to the host, be sure to do a little research. While it may go
without saying, make sure that their podcast is active and that they do indeed
host guests. Listen to a few episodes so that you can understand how you can be
a valuable guest. Pay attention to learn how you can be relatable to their
audience and make note of any potential talking points.
STEP 2 	Do Some Basic Research
© 2022 Cardone Training Technologies, Inc. All rights reserved.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 56 of 65 --

57
There are multiple ways to reach out, but generally email is a great place to start.
Sometimes podcasts will even have an application on their website. Keep in
mind that with bigger shows, you will be reaching a gatekeeper. The key is to
have a short customized message that makes it clear how you can provide their
audience value. Include your media kit if you have one.
STEP 3 	Contact to Request an Interview
As Grant always says, “the follow up is where the gold is.” It is very common to
not get a reply to your first message. What you need to do is show persistence,
while at the same time not being annoying. If you do it right, people will often
respect your hustle. Follow up every week or two, and always come from a fresh
angle. Try using different channels including social media.
STEP 4 	Follow Up Until You Book
While it will often take some back and forth to get a date in the books, once
you’re in contact you should assume you’re going to land the gig and begin
preparing. Get familiar with the format of the host’s show and some of the
common questions they ask. Come up with some good talking points and a few
potential stories. Also know how to best position what you offer.
STEP 5 	Prepare For Your Interview
Hey [Name]!
I’m a big fan of the show. I just listened to [episode name] and loved [fact
about show].
If you are looking for quality guests for future episodes, I’d love to be on.
I’m a [area of expertise] who helps people [problem you solve]. Having listened
to your show, I think we’d have a very entertaining discussion that would
provide a ton of value for your audience.
Maybe we could cover topics like [topic idea 1] or [ topic idea 2].
If you think this type of information could be beneficial to your listeners, I’m
happy to send samples of [appearances or credibility].
A quick reply either way would mean a lot. Thanks!
[Your Name]
New Message

Anchor is an all-in-one hosting option that gives you access to Spotify’s
ad network. Anchor allows you to dynamically insert paid ads into your
episodes, even with zero technical skill. Requiring a minimum of only 50

revenue with minimal effort. Learn more at 10xresource.com/anchor
MONETIZING YOUR PODCAST
Once your podcast is up and running there, there are a lot of different ways to monetize.
Most podcasts will deploy a combination of the following:
Probably the most common method to earn from a podcast, ads are usually stitched
into your podcast audio in post production (pre-roll or mid-roll). While you can sell ads
directly, there are advantages to using a podcast ad network. In addition to saving you
time, networks such as AdvertiseCast allow advertisers to find you. The only caveat is
that you often need a well-established listener base.
What can you expect to be paid? Ads are generally priced as CPM, which is the cost an
advertiser pays per thousand listeners. Pricing will depend on your niche, as well as the
length and placement of the ad. You can expect to earn anywhere between $10 to $30
per thousand listeners per ad.
MONETIZATION STRATEGIES
ADVERTISEMENTS
While sponsorships can look similar to ads, they often are much more profitable and
deeper relationships. You’ve heard podcast hosts promote sponsors by saying
something like, “go to websitename.com/joe to get an exclusive discount”. Additional
perks for sponsors can include links on your website or email newsletter. Big sponsors
may even pay to get their logo on your podcast cover art.
SPONSORSHIPS
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Earning money from your podcast is great, but your primary
goal is to build your subscriber list. Create a free “lead magnet”
and have an easy to remember URL to send people to.
Grant uses Kajabi to deliver all of this training to individual users. While
the primary focus of Kajabi is memberships and courses, it is an all-in-one
solution that includes websites, funnels, email and more. It is ideal if you
need a way to easily collect money and deliver digital content. Try it free
at 10xresource.com/kajabi
What can you expect to be paid? The amount will vary widely, again depending on
your niche and your download stats. A sponsorship will generally pay a negotiated
amount and will often run for multiple episodes. Even as a small show you can demand
$1000. When you get big, it could be millions.
Depending on your niche and audience, you may monetize by offering paid courses or
recurring membership. Premium monthly subscriptions can be managed on many of
the platforms you distribute your podcast, such as Apple Podcasts, Spotify or YouTube.
This is ideal for “behind the scenes” or exclusive content. If you sell courses, you will likely
use a third-party platform to deliver training materials.
What can you expect to be paid? Monthly memberships can range from $5 a month
to $100+. Courses also range widely, anywhere from $10 to $2000. Pricing all comes
down to the value the users receive. Fun content for fans will be on the low end, while
you can charge more for business systems.
PREMIUM CONTENT
A popular way for creators to build their brand and generate income is to sell “merch”.
Grant built his ecommerce empire using Shopify (store.grantcardone.com) and now has
an entire fulfillment center. Luckily, thanks to print-on-demand, you don’t need
inventory or a warehouse. Sites like Teespring, Merchlabs and Printful allow you to be
selling a variety of clothing and merchandise in minutes.
BRANDED MERCHANDISE

I am huge on branded merchandise. We sell everything
from 10X day planners to 10X cufflinks. There is no better
advertising in the world than a real fan wearing your stuff.
What can you expect to be paid? Most print-on-demand sites allow you to set your
own pricing, and therefore your own profit margins. Say for example the base price for a
tee shirt is $10 and you sell it for $25. Your profit would be $15. Some sites differ on their
pricing model and will vary by product type.
One of the fastest ways to monetize your podcast is by promoting affiliate products and
service. As an affiliate, you earn a commission based on the sales referral. All sales are
tracked by the use of your unique affiliate link. It is easy to apply and get approved as an
affiliate. Many companies host their affiliate program on large affiliate networks, while
others have their own affiliate programs (like us!)
What can you expect to be paid? The commission percentage will vary depending on
the project. Generally speaking, digital products pay a higher commission than physical
products. For example, Clickfunnels pays upwards of 40% on their monthly subscription.
Amazon pays only 4.5% on books.
AFFILIATE PRODUCTS
It is actually quite common for podcast creators to ask their listeners to support the
show financially. You can easily collect one-time donations using Paypal. What has
become extremely popular though is having people make small monthly contributions.
This is done through a website called Patreon, which allows you to provide supporters
with special perks in return for being a patron.

I now charge $100,000 or more even for a few hours of consulting.
While you won’t start there, don’t be afraid to charge for your

What can you expect to be paid? You will want to create a variety of levels on Patreon,

The more you establish yourself as an expert, the more you increase your ability to be
paid for your knowledge. There are many ways that you can structure this type of
exchange. Coaching is usually with individuals and consulting is usually with companies.
You can do contract work for as little as an hour or structure an ongoing retainer
relationship. It can be on-site, virtual, or a combination of the two.
What can you expect to be paid? Ultimately your price comes down to the value you
deliver. If you can help a company increase their revenue by millions, then you can
demand $1000 an hour. On the other hand, you might charge $500 a month if you’re
providing services that won’t produce an ROI.
COACHING AND CONSULTING
